answer:
Designing Idempotency for Production APIs

1. Definition & Context

Idempotency means that performing an operation multiple times produces the same result as performing it once. HTTP GET, PUT, and DELETE are inherently idempotent by specification; POST is not. In production APIs, idempotency is critical for preventing duplicate side effects when clients retry requests due to network timeouts, load balancer retries, or user double-clicks.

Implementation strategies: idempotency keys (client-generated UUID sent in headers), natural idempotency (SET operations like UPDATE balance = 100 vs UPDATE balance = balance - 10), and conditional writes (ETags, version numbers, IF NOT EXISTS).

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the leave approval endpoint needed to be idempotent. A manager clicking "Approve" twice (due to slow network) shouldn't send two approval notifications or decrement the balance twice. Manoj designed the approval as a naturally idempotent operation: UPDATE leaves SET status = 'approved' WHERE id = ? AND status = 'pending' — the WHERE clause ensures the second call finds no rows to update (status is already 'approved'), making it a no-op.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// Natural idempotency for leave approval (HR Leave System)
async function approveLeave(leaveId: string, approverId: string) {
  return prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    // Only update if still pending — idempotent by design
    const result = await tx.leave.updateMany({
      where: { id: leaveId, status: 'pending' },
      data: {
        status: 'approved',
        approvedBy: approverId,
        approvedAt: new Date()
      }
    });
    
    if (result.count === 0) {
      // Already processed — return current state (idempotent response)
      const existing = await tx.leave.findUnique({ where: { id: leaveId } });
      if (existing?.status === 'approved') {
        return existing; // Same result as first call
      }
      throw new Error('Leave not found or already rejected');
    }
    
    // Only decrement balance on first successful approval
    const leave = await tx.leave.findUnique({ where: { id: leaveId } });
    const days = calculateBusinessDays(leave.startDate, leave.endDate);
    await tx.leaveBalance.update({
      where: { userId: leave.userId },
      data: { remaining: { decrement: days } }
    });
    
    return leave;
  });
}

// Idempotency key for CSV import (ContactsHub)
const processedKeys = new Map();

fastify.post('/api/contacts/import', async (request, reply) => {
  const key = request.headers['x-idempotency-key'];
  if (key && processedKeys.has(key)) {
    return reply.code(200).send(processedKeys.get(key));
  }
  
  const result = await importContacts(request.body);
  if (key) processedKeys.set(key, result);
  reply.code(201).send(result);
});

5. Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

Symptom: A manager reported that an employee received two Slack notifications for the same leave approval. The leave balance was correctly decremented only once, but the notification was sent twice.

Investigation: Manoj checked the request logs and found two PATCH requests 500ms apart (manager double-clicked). The database update was idempotent (second call returned the already-approved leave), but the notification was sent outside the idempotency check.

Root Cause: The notification was sent after the database transaction, regardless of whether the status actually changed. Both requests passed the transaction (first updated, second returned existing), and both triggered io.emit('leave:approved').

Fix: Manoj moved the notification inside the conditional: only emit when result.count > 0 (actual state change occurred). For the idempotent case (already approved), the response was returned without triggering side effects.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Side effects (email, notification) sent on retryGuard side effects behind state-change check
Idempotency key storage fills memoryUse Redis with TTL (24h) instead of in-memory Map
Relative operations (balance -= 10) not idempotentUse absolute SET or conditional WHERE clause
Client forgets to send idempotency keyRequire key for POST endpoints via middleware

7. Validation Strategy

  • Double-submit test: Call approval twice in rapid succession — verify single balance decrement
  • Side effect test: Mock notification service — assert exactly 1 notification sent
  • Key collision test: Same idempotency key with different payload — should return first result
  • Expiry test: After TTL expires, same key should process as new request

Debugging Idempotency Issues

1. Definition & Context

Idempotency bugs are among the most dangerous in production systems because they cause real-world damage: duplicate charges, duplicate records, double-sent emails. They're hard to detect in testing because they require specific timing (concurrent requests, network retries) and often pass single-threaded unit tests perfectly.

Common patterns: non-idempotent POST endpoints (each call creates a new resource), side effects outside transaction boundaries (notification sent even when DB write fails), and race conditions between check and write (read balance → approve → write balance, without locking).

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the bulk CSV import endpoint created 500 contacts per batch. When Render's load balancer detected a 30-second timeout, it retried the request to a different server instance. The first instance was still processing, so both completed — creating 1000 contacts instead of 500.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// Fix: Distributed idempotency lock using MongoDB (ContactsHub)
// idempotency-keys collection with unique index on 'key'
const IdempotencyKey = mongoose.model('IdempotencyKey', new mongoose.Schema({
  key: { type: String, unique: true, required: true },
  status: { type: String, enum: ['processing', 'completed'], default: 'processing' },
  response: { type: mongoose.Schema.Types.Mixed },
  createdAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now, expires: 86400 } // TTL: 24h
}));

async function idempotentImport(req, res) {
  const key = req.headers['x-idempotency-key'];
  if (!key) return res.status(400).json({ error: 'X-Idempotency-Key required' });
  
  try {
    // Atomic upsert — only one server wins the insert
    await IdempotencyKey.create({ key, status: 'processing' });
  } catch (err) {
    if (err.code === 11000) { // Duplicate key
      // Another server is processing or has completed
      const existing = await IdempotencyKey.findOne({ key });
      if (existing.status === 'completed') {
        return res.status(200).json(existing.response);
      }
      // Still processing — return 409 to prevent duplicate
      return res.status(409).json({ error: 'Import in progress' });
    }
    throw err;
  }
  
  // This server wins — process the import
  const result = await importContacts(req.body);
  await IdempotencyKey.updateOne(
    { key },
    { status: 'completed', response: result }
  );
  return res.status(201).json(result);
}

5. Debugging Narrative — ContactsHub

Symptom: After a slow import (35 seconds for 500 contacts), the user saw 1000 contacts in the list. The import progress bar showed 100% and a success message, but the data was doubled.

Investigation: Manoj checked the MongoDB contacts collection and found exact duplicate pairs (same name, email, phone) created 30 seconds apart. Render's access logs showed two POST requests to /api/contacts/import — the first at T+0, the second at T+30 (load balancer retry).

Root Cause: Render's default request timeout was 30 seconds. When the import took 35 seconds, the load balancer assumed the request failed and retried to a different instance. Both instances ran the import independently.

Fix: Three changes: (1) Added idempotency key stored in MongoDB (survives across server instances). (2) Increased Render's request timeout to 120 seconds for the import endpoint. (3) Switched to a background job pattern: POST returns 202 Accepted with a job ID, client polls GET /api/imports/:jobId for status.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
In-memory idempotency key (lost on restart)Store in database with TTL index
Load balancer retry creates duplicatesAdd distributed lock via unique DB constraint
Long-running request times out at proxyUse async job pattern: 202 + polling
Race between two servers checking same keyUse atomic INSERT with unique constraint, not read-then-write

7. Validation Strategy

  • Concurrent import test: Fire 5 identical imports simultaneously — verify exactly 500 contacts created
  • Timeout simulation: Set 1-second timeout on test client, verify no duplicates on retry
  • Multi-instance test: Run 2 server instances, send same import to both — verify deduplication
  • TTL test: Verify idempotency key expires after 24 hours, allowing re-import

Debugging Consistency Model Issues

1. Definition & Context

Consistency models define the guarantees a system provides about the order and visibility of operations. Key models: Strong consistency (reads always return latest write — linearizability), Eventual consistency (reads may return stale data but will converge), Read-your-writes (a client always sees its own writes), Causal consistency (causally related operations appear in order). Most bugs arise from developers assuming strong consistency when the system provides eventual consistency.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, the lead status update flow had a consistency bug. After a sales rep updated a lead from "New" to "Contacted" and immediately navigated to the dashboard, the dashboard still showed the lead as "New". The update was written to the primary MongoDB node, but the dashboard read from a secondary replica that hadn't replicated yet.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// MongoDB read concern for consistency (CRM Dashboard)
import { MongoClient, ReadPreference } from 'mongodb';

// Default: read from secondary (fast, eventually consistent)
const defaultCollection = db.collection('leads');

// After write: read from primary (strong, slower)
async function updateLeadStatus(leadId: string, newStatus: string) {
  // Write to primary
  await db.collection('leads').updateOne(
    { _id: leadId },
    { $set: { status: newStatus, updatedAt: new Date() } },
    { writeConcern: { w: 'majority' } } // Wait for majority ack
  );
  
  // Read back from primary — read-your-writes guarantee
  const updated = await db.collection('leads').findOne(
    { _id: leadId },
    { readPreference: ReadPreference.PRIMARY }
  );
  
  return updated;
}

// Mongoose session-based causal consistency
async function updateAndFetchDashboard(userId: string, leadId: string, status: string) {
  const session = await mongoose.startSession();
  session.startTransaction({
    readConcern: { level: 'majority' },
    writeConcern: { w: 'majority' },
    readPreference: 'primary'
  });
  
  await Lead.updateOne({ _id: leadId }, { status }, { session });
  const dashboardData = await Lead.find({ assignedTo: userId }, null, { session });
  
  await session.commitTransaction();
  return dashboardData; // Guaranteed to include the update
}

5. Debugging Narrative — CRM Dashboard

Symptom: Sales reps complained that after updating a lead status, going back to the dashboard showed the old status. Refreshing the page after 2-3 seconds showed the correct status.

Investigation: Manoj added a readPreference log to the Mongoose connection. Dashboard reads used secondaryPreferred (configured for performance). The MongoDB replica set had a primary in Mumbai and a secondary in the same region with ~200ms replication lag.

Root Cause: The dashboard GET endpoint read from the secondary replica for load distribution. After a write to the primary, the 200ms replication lag meant the secondary still had stale data when the user navigated to the dashboard.

Fix: Manoj implemented a read-your-writes pattern: after any mutation, the frontend stored a lastWriteTimestamp in sessionStorage. Dashboard API requests included this timestamp as a query parameter. The server middleware detected it and switched to readPreference: 'primary' for requests within a 5-second window of the write, then reverted to secondary reads for older data.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Secondary reads show stale data after writesUse primary reads for user's own data after mutation
All reads from primary (defeats replica purpose)Only force primary reads within a short window after write
Causal consistency not supported in older MongoDBUse MongoDB 3.6+ with sessions for causal guarantees
UI shows stale then jumps to fresh (confusing UX)Use optimistic UI updates — show expected state immediately

7. Validation Strategy

  • Stale read test: Write to primary, immediately read from secondary — measure lag
  • Read-your-writes test: Update lead, fetch dashboard — verify updated status appears
  • Failover test: Kill primary, verify secondary promotes and reads continue
  • Optimistic UI test: Verify UI shows expected state before server confirms

Comparing Consistency Model Approaches

1. Definition & Context

The two main approaches to consistency in web applications are strong consistency (every read returns the latest write, using transactions and primary reads) and eventual consistency (reads may return stale data, but the system converges over time). The trade-off is latency and availability vs correctness.

Modern approaches blend both: per-operation consistency (strong for critical paths like payments, eventual for analytics), optimistic UI (show expected result immediately, reconcile with server later), and CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types for automatic conflict resolution).

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System vs CRM Dashboard

In Manoj's HR Leave System, leave balance operations used strong consistency (Serializable transactions in PostgreSQL) because double-deducting a balance had real-world impact. In the CRM Dashboard, the sales pipeline chart used eventual consistency — if a lead moved from "Qualified" to "Proposal" and the chart showed the old count for 2 seconds, it was acceptable because the chart auto-refreshed every 30 seconds and no financial decision depended on instant accuracy.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// Approach A: Strong Consistency (HR Leave System — PostgreSQL)
async function deductBalance(userId: string, days: number) {
  return prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    const balance = await tx.$queryRaw`
      SELECT remaining FROM leave_balances 
      WHERE user_id = ${userId} FOR UPDATE
    `;
    if (balance[0].remaining < days) {
      throw new Error('Insufficient balance');
    }
    await tx.leaveBalance.update({
      where: { userId },
      data: { remaining: { decrement: days } }
    });
  }, { isolationLevel: 'Serializable' });
}

// Approach B: Eventual Consistency + Optimistic UI (CRM Dashboard)
// Server: Read from replica for dashboard
app.get('/api/dashboard/pipeline', async (req, res) => {
  const pipeline = await Lead.aggregate([
    { $group: { _id: '$status', count: { $sum: 1 } } }
  ]).read('secondaryPreferred'); // Fast, may be slightly stale
  res.json(pipeline);
});

// Client: Optimistic update after status change
function updateLeadStatus(leadId, newStatus) {
  // Immediately update local state (optimistic)
  dispatch({ type: 'LEAD_STATUS_UPDATED', leadId, status: newStatus });
  
  // Fire API call in background
  api.patch(`/leads/${leadId}`, { status: newStatus })
    .catch(() => {
      // Rollback optimistic update on failure
      dispatch({ type: 'LEAD_STATUS_ROLLBACK', leadId });
    });
}

5. Debugging Narrative — CRM Dashboard

Symptom: The pipeline chart showed incorrect numbers after a sales meeting where 5 leads were moved to "Won" status in quick succession. The total leads count in the sidebar said 50, but the chart segments only summed to 48.

Root Cause: The sidebar count read from primary (it was a simple count query), but the pipeline aggregation read from secondary. Two of the 5 status updates hadn't replicated yet, creating a count mismatch.

Fix: Manoj standardized dashboard reads: all widgets on the same page read from the same source (secondaryPreferred) with a shared cache key. He added an auto-refresh every 30 seconds and a "Data updated 5s ago" indicator. For the sidebar, he switched from a primary read to using the same cached aggregation result, ensuring internal consistency even if slightly stale.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Mixing consistency levels on same pageAll widgets on a page should use same read source
Optimistic UI not rolling back on failureImplement error handler that reverts local state
Strong consistency everywhere = slow appClassify operations: only critical paths need strong
User confused by stale dataShow "last updated" timestamp; auto-refresh

7. Validation Strategy

  • Manoj's choice: Strong for balance/approval, eventual for dashboards/analytics
  • Consistency test: Update record, read from both primary and secondary — measure convergence time
  • Widget consistency test: All dashboard widgets should show internally consistent data
  • Optimistic UI test: Simulate network failure after optimistic update — verify rollback

Comparing Concurrency Control Approaches

1. Definition & Context

Concurrency control manages simultaneous access to shared resources to prevent data corruption. Two main approaches: Pessimistic concurrency (lock the resource before modifying — SELECT ... FOR UPDATE, database-level locks) and Optimistic concurrency (allow concurrent access, detect conflicts at write time using version numbers or timestamps). The choice depends on contention frequency: pessimistic for high contention (many writers), optimistic for low contention (mostly reads).

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System vs ContactsHub

In Manoj's HR Leave System, leave balance updates used pessimistic locking (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) because concurrent approvals for the same employee were common during end-of-quarter rushes — 3-4 managers might approve leaves simultaneously. In ContactsHub, contact editing used optimistic concurrency with Mongoose's version field (__v) because concurrent edits to the same contact were rare (personal contact manager), and locking would unnecessarily slow reads.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// Approach A: Pessimistic — PostgreSQL FOR UPDATE (HR Leave System)
async function approveWithLock(leaveId: string) {
  return prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    // Acquire row-level lock — blocks other transactions
    const [balance] = await tx.$queryRaw`
      SELECT * FROM leave_balances
      WHERE user_id = (SELECT user_id FROM leaves WHERE id = ${leaveId})
      FOR UPDATE NOWAIT  -- Fail fast instead of waiting
    `;
    // ... safely modify balance ...
  });
}

// Approach B: Optimistic — Mongoose version check (ContactsHub)
async function updateContact(id: string, updates: Partial, expectedVersion: number) {
  const result = await Contact.findOneAndUpdate(
    { _id: id, __v: expectedVersion },  // Version must match
    { $set: updates, $inc: { __v: 1 } },
    { new: true }
  );
  
  if (!result) {
    // Version mismatch — someone else modified it
    const current = await Contact.findById(id);
    throw {
      status: 409,
      message: 'Contact was modified by another user',
      currentVersion: current.__v,
      currentData: current
    };
  }
  return result;
}

// Client-side retry for optimistic concurrency
async function saveWithRetry(contact, maxRetries = 3) {
  for (let i = 0; i < maxRetries; i++) {
    try {
      return await api.patch(`/contacts/${contact.id}`, {
        ...contact.changes,
        __v: contact.__v
      });
    } catch (err) {
      if (err.status === 409 && i < maxRetries - 1) {
        // Refresh and merge — auto-resolve if no field conflicts
        contact = await api.get(`/contacts/${contact.id}`);
        continue;
      }
      throw err;
    }
  }
}

5. Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

Symptom: During the December rush, 3 managers trying to approve leaves simultaneously experienced a 10-second hang, then one got a "transaction timeout" error.

Investigation: Manoj checked PostgreSQL's pg_locks view and found a deadlock: Transaction A locked balance row for Employee 1, then tried to lock balance for Employee 2. Transaction B locked Employee 2's balance, then tried Employee 1's. Classic deadlock.

Root Cause: The approval endpoint sometimes processed batch approvals (approve 3 leaves at once), and the lock acquisition order wasn't consistent across transactions.

Fix: Manoj ensured consistent lock ordering by sorting employee IDs before acquiring locks: ORDER BY user_id ASC. He also added FOR UPDATE NOWAIT to fail fast instead of waiting indefinitely, and wrapped the entire batch in a single transaction with retry logic.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Deadlocks from inconsistent lock orderingAlways acquire locks in a deterministic order (sorted ID)
Optimistic retry storm under high contentionAdd exponential backoff; switch to pessimistic if conflict rate >10%
Forgot to check version on updateUse middleware that enforces version check for all PATCH endpoints
Lock held too long (long-running transaction)Keep transactions short; do I/O outside transaction

7. Validation Strategy

  • Manoj's choice: Pessimistic for financial data (balance), optimistic for user content (contacts)
  • Deadlock test: Simulate 10 concurrent approvals with overlapping employees
  • Version conflict test: Two clients edit same contact — verify 409 on second save
  • Performance test: Measure throughput: pessimistic (locks) vs optimistic (retries) under load

Designing Concurrency Control for Production

1. Definition & Context

Designing concurrency control for a mid-size product involves choosing the right strategy per resource, implementing it correctly in the data layer, and exposing appropriate error semantics to the client. The design must consider: contention patterns (how often do multiple users modify the same resource?), conflict cost (what happens if a conflict slips through?), and user experience (how to communicate conflicts gracefully).

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, he designed a layered concurrency control strategy: (1) Database level: PostgreSQL Serializable transactions with FOR UPDATE on the leave_balances table. (2) Application level: Idempotent approval endpoint using status check (WHERE status = 'pending'). (3) UI level: Disable the "Approve" button immediately on click with a loading spinner, preventing accidental double-clicks. (4) WebSocket level: Broadcast status changes via Socket.io so other managers see the leave as "Approved" before they try to approve it too.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// Full layered concurrency control (HR Leave System)

// Layer 1: Angular component — UI-level protection
@Component({ template: `
  
`})
export class LeaveApprovalComponent {
  isApproving = false;
  
  async approve(leaveId: string) {
    this.isApproving = true;  // Prevent double-click
    try {
      await this.leaveService.approve(leaveId);
    } catch (err) {
      if (err.status === 409) {
        this.snackbar.open('Already processed by another manager');
      }
    } finally {
      this.isApproving = false;
    }
  }
}

// Layer 2: Socket.io broadcast after successful approval
io.on('connection', (socket) => {
  socket.join(`department:${socket.user.departmentId}`);
});

async function approveLeave(leaveId, approverId) {
  const result = await approveWithLock(leaveId, approverId);
  // Broadcast to all managers in department
  io.to(`department:${result.departmentId}`).emit('leave:status-changed', {
    leaveId,
    status: 'approved',
    approvedBy: approverId
  });
  return result;
}

// Layer 3+4: API + Database (combined)
async function approveWithLock(leaveId: string, approverId: string) {
  return prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    const leave = await tx.leave.findFirst({
      where: { id: leaveId, status: 'pending' }  // Idempotent check
    });
    if (!leave) {
      throw { status: 409, message: 'Leave already processed' };
    }
    
    await tx.$queryRaw`
      SELECT 1 FROM leave_balances
      WHERE user_id = ${leave.userId}
      FOR UPDATE
    `;
    
    const days = calculateBusinessDays(leave.startDate, leave.endDate);
    await tx.leaveBalance.update({
      where: { userId: leave.userId },
      data: { remaining: { decrement: days } }
    });
    
    return tx.leave.update({
      where: { id: leaveId },
      data: { status: 'approved', approvedBy: approverId }
    });
  }, { isolationLevel: 'Serializable' });
}

5. Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

Symptom: Despite all four layers, a race condition still occurred: two managers approved the same leave within 50ms. The Socket.io broadcast arrived too late to prevent the second click.

Investigation: Manoj's logs showed both PATCH requests hit the server 50ms apart. The UI disable was working (each manager only clicked once), but they clicked almost simultaneously from different browsers.

Root Cause: Layers 1 and 2 are best-effort — they reduce but don't eliminate concurrent requests. The database layer (Layer 4) was the actual safety net and worked correctly: the second transaction waited for the first to commit (due to FOR UPDATE), then found status = 'approved' instead of 'pending', and returned 409.

Lesson: UI and WebSocket layers reduce contention (fewer conflicts reach the database), but the database layer must be the source of truth. Defense in depth works: upper layers improve UX, lower layers guarantee correctness.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Relying only on UI-level preventionAlways enforce at database level — UI is client-side and bypassable
Socket.io event arrives after competing clickAccept that WebSocket is best-effort; database is the safety net
Long transactions block other operationsKeep lock scope minimal; only lock the specific row needed
Error message unclear to user on 409Show "Already approved by [name]" with current state

7. Validation Strategy

  • Race condition test: Script 5 concurrent approvals — assert exactly 1 succeeds, 4 get 409
  • UI test: Playwright — click Approve, verify button disables within 1 frame
  • Socket test: Two browser windows — approve in one, verify status updates in other within 500ms
  • Load test: 50 concurrent users approving different leaves — verify no deadlocks
